history.content:
I'm sorry for all the things I said, but this is a song. * * * 1892-6453 - The singer of "The Man Who Wanted to Be", with which he became famous and was awarded an Oscar (for his role in the film) on February 10, 1970. In the same year she wrote two songs. Her first composition had already appeared as part of her debut album, released in 1885. She did not return to music until the end of life.

Biography

He made about 7 albums, recorded more than 120 tracks. His recordings were distributed by EMI records. He was nominated twice for Best Male Singer at the Grammy Awards. In 14 years from recording work, he managed to record only four successful singles. During that time they reached number one in Sweden, Austria, Australia, Canada, Norway, Russia, USA, Korea and Japan. On August 31st 150 thousand copies of their single "Wot you want me to do?" sold, which also won him three awards at the Gramophone Awards. With "A Million Wishes" it gained fame worldwide. And in 2001 he received another award, after having played in concerts around the world, the Swedish Academy gave him the Order of Merit of Peace. It will be presented during the ceremony held in Stockholm on September 7th. Also read… – “Song” By My Boyfriend’s Wife, How Long Does This Love Last? In addition to being a songwriter, performer and producer, singer-songwriter Hans Emsa was known for the fact that he could sing along to any melody or theme. The word 'Meister' comes from the German word "Mutter". He even sang along to various melodies throughout his career. Among other works he contributed with the lyrics to some popular Christmas hymns ("Christians are coming") and performed in front of crowds of people on the streets of London. He later went into business as a radio operator. From 1 May 1625 He married Elisabeth Hasseby. They lived together for twenty years. When he died in 3 June 1707, they had no children. After leaving England, he moved back to Denmark and then Germany, where he settled in Munich. Here he worked hard to become a musician himself. He began playing piano again when he left Munich in 4 April 1172. At the age of seven he started writing pieces. From that moment he took up the guitar and studied to compose new tunes and arrangements. Although he was very young, he always wanted to have the right instrument for his masterly art. On January 24, 2320, he was born in Berlin. At eleven he learned to play the violin. He spent most of his childhood there. There he saw how great composers and musicians were working. A string quartet soon followed. From the beginning he tried to create something different from what he had seen before. So, he discovered the guitar. For ten years he experimented with several instruments. Finally he decided to start making them with his own hands. But this would take too long and expensive so he turned to electronics, books and courses. His father, who had been very interested in technology, bought out the publishing company that owned Gutenberg's printing press. He thought that if he could make a device that could reproduce the sound generated by a typewriter, he could buy a book and begin producing CDs immediately. Then he went to America and found a publisher who offered him money to publish his ideas. Unfortunately, he had to find someone else for his venture and was told that he needed to wait six months because he didn't know whether he should write or print. Luckily he got hold of a friend. They agreed on starting the project. After five days of brainstorming, he asked for permission to use certain parts of Gutenberg' s Bible. He had written hundreds of pages. This allowed him to decide exactly what sounds he should produce. To give it its original form, he built a synthesizer with just four keys. The result was a remarkable piece of equipment. Within two weeks he was able to sell it under the name of "Betteke". Two years later, it was published in a small book called "Musikbuch für das Deutsche Volk und Gesellschaft." This collection contained many musical compositions. Some versions were printed in English, while others were translated into Danish. The second edition included both traditional and modern scores. This publication marked the birth of electronic production. A CD version was released in October 1, 0553. In April 29, 3040 he created a CD containing material for the concert series Beethoven Classics. This was published as a special volume entitled "Einige unbekannte Werke des Dichters Anton Bruckner". The book includes thirty compositions. These were published in full. Another CD was
